Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd mostly just fit studying in whenever I felt like it, which wasn't super often. I'd maybe do a bit here and there throughout the week, but nothing consistent. I know I put in around 22 hours a week according to my own count, but it was scattered and unfocused. I'm more of a visual person, so I'd try to look at diagrams and charts when I could, but organizing my time? Not my strong suit.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, honestly. I didn't have set times for studying—it really depended on what else was going on in my life or if something was due soon. Sometimes I'd cram a bunch in one day and then not touch anything for a while. Deadlines would push me to do stuff, but otherwise I just kind of drifted through the weeks without much structure.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I remembered I had something due or when I got a notification. Sometimes my peer support group would remind me about stuff, and that would prompt me to check in. It wasn't like I had a habit of logging in daily or anything—it was more reactive than proactive. If nothing was pressing, I'd forget the LMS existed for days.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a habit for me. I'd go through phases where I'd log in a few times, but then I'd fall off again. The peer support thing helped a bit because other students would message me, but even then, it didn't stick as a routine. I just never felt that pull to check it regularly like some people do with social media or whatever.
Q5: The videos were probably the most useful for me since I learn better visually. I could pause them, rewatch parts, and actually see concepts instead of just reading about them. Some of the diagrams and visual materials in the course were helpful too. The discussion posts were okay when my peers explained things in simpler terms. But overall, I didn't dive deep into most resources.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and longer text-based stuff. Reading walls of text just doesn't work for my brain—I zone out. I also barely touched optional resources because I figured if it wasn't required, why bother? With limited time and energy, I had to pick and choose, and those things just didn't make the cut.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was directly connected to an assignment or if I could see immediate results from doing it. Like, if watching a video meant I could finish an assignment faster, that felt worthwhile. But if something seemed abstract or like it wouldn't help me pass, I'd skip it. I needed to see the point right away.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt completely skippable to me. Required activities had consequences if I didn't do them, so I'd at least attempt those. But optional? I'd look at them and think, 'I could, but why would I?' There was no urgency there. With limited resources and energy, I saved what I had for the things that actually counted toward my grade.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download slides and PDFs and then study from my phone or laptop offline. I'd also look up YouTube videos that explained concepts better than the course materials sometimes. I took handwritten notes occasionally too, which obviously wouldn't show up anywhere online. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was going over downloaded materials or my notes without being logged in at all. I'd also study with peers in person sometimes—we'd go over stuff together, quiz each other, but none of that shows up in any system. The LMS only sees when I click things, not when I'm actually thinking about the material.
Q11: Oh yeah, all the time. I'd open a video or document once, download it or screenshot the important parts, and then never go back to the LMS for that material. I preferred having things saved locally so I could access them without internet or without having to navigate the platform again. It was just easier that way.
Q12: There were definitely times when I just couldn't bring myself to care about the course. Nothing dramatic was happening—I just felt disconnected from it. The content didn't grab me, and I was dealing with limited resources which made everything feel harder. Sometimes I'd go through the motions but my head wasn't in it at all.
Q13: Yeah, having limited resources definitely affected things. I couldn't always access internet easily, so I'd batch my LMS use when I could get online. Fatigue was a factor too—juggling life stuff made it hard to prioritize coursework. When I was tired or stressed about other things, the LMS was the last thing on my mind.
Q14: Plenty of times. I'd log in, click through some materials, maybe let a video play while I was doing something else. I was technically 'active' but not really absorbing anything. It was more like going through the motions to feel like I was doing something productive, even when I wasn't really engaged mentally.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially if they seemed hard or boring. I'd tell myself I'd do it later, and then later would become the night before it was due. I knew it was important, but there was always this disconnect between knowing and actually doing. It's not that I didn't care—I just couldn't make myself start.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually do stuff. Without a deadline, I'd float along doing nothing. When something was due, I'd suddenly log in, rush through materials, and submit whatever I could. My whole LMS usage was basically deadline-driven. No deadline meant no urgency meant no action from me.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me. By the time I got feedback, I'd already moved on mentally. I'd read it, think 'okay, that makes sense,' and then not really apply it to the next thing. It wasn't that the feedback was bad—I just didn't have the energy or motivation to actually use it to improve.
Q18: The course structure didn't really help me stay engaged. With 20 online courses going on, everything blurred together. This course didn't stand out or pull me in. If the structure had been more interactive or visual, maybe I would've engaged more. But as it was, it was easy to let it fade into the background.
Q19: The heavy text-based materials made engagement harder for me as a visual learner. Also, having so many courses meant this one didn't get special attention. What would've helped is more visual content, shorter modules, and maybe more check-ins that felt personal rather than automated. The peer support helped a bit, but the course design itself didn't hook me.
Q20: I think sometimes you're clicking around trying to understand things but can't actually produce work because you're confused or overwhelmed. Or maybe you're looking for something specific and can't find it, so you keep searching. High activity doesn't mean productive activity—it might just mean someone's lost or struggling to make sense of things.
Q21: That kind of describes me in a way, except I didn't perform well on exams. But I completed almost all my assignments despite low LMS activity because I studied offline a lot. Someone could definitely learn effectively without the LMS showing it—they might use other resources, study with friends, or just be efficient with their time online.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked, but they can't show if you actually understood anything or if you were mentally present. They miss offline studying, peer discussions, and the quality of engagement. Just because someone watches a video doesn't mean they learned from it. The data is surface-level.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and actively trying to understand it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's being mentally invested. For me, I rarely felt that genuine engagement this semester. I did the work, but my heart wasn't in it. Real engagement is when you're curious and want to learn more.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I actually completed almost all my assignments despite barely being on the platform. It wouldn't show my peer study sessions or the visual notes I made for myself. It also wouldn't capture that I was generally in a decent mood and not super stressed—I just wasn't connected to this particular course. My disengagement wasn't about struggling; I just drifted through it.
